files.name: fw.algo
files.content:
buildTable()

for each a in nodes() do
  seedRow(a)
end

for each k in nodes() do
  relaxThrough(k)
end

report()

notes.text: **Build the empty table.** `buildTable` numbers the vertices `0…n−1`, creates an `n × n` grid of zeros, and names its rows and columns after the vertices — nothing seeded yet. The matrix is the whole story of Floyd–Warshall, so watch it in the panel.

**Seed the base case, one row per step.** For each vertex `a` we fill its row with the *direct* distances: `0` to itself, the edge weight to a neighbour, and `∞` to everyone else. Step through it and watch the table fill row by row.

The one idea behind Floyd–Warshall: take each vertex `k` in turn as an allowed **intermediate**. After `k` vertices have been allowed, `dist[i][j]` is the shortest path that only detours through them; allow them all and every entry becomes a true shortest distance.

`relaxThrough(k)` is the inner double loop, kept in a helper: for **every pair** `(i, j)`, check whether `i → k → j` beats the best path so far. The cells that improve blink in the panel — one DP wave per `k`.
No path to trace — the filled **matrix** *is* the answer: `dist[i][j]` is the shortest distance from vertex `i` to vertex `j`, for **all** pairs at once.

files.name: fw-helpers.algo
files.content:
export function buildTable() do
  idx ← scratch.createMap("idx")            // vertex → row/col (hidden bookkeeping)
  n ← 0
  for each v in nodes() do
    idx[v] ← n                              // number the vertices 0, 1, 2, …
    n ← n + 1
  end
  dist ← panel.createMatrix(n, n, "dist")   // an empty n × n table
  dist.setLabels(nodes())                   // name rows & columns by vertex (A, B, …)
  note(dist, "shortest so far")
  pin(dist)                                 // keep the table on top
  spotlight(dist)                           // the matrix is the star of Floyd–Warshall
  showMessage("Empty table — seed the base case next", "info")
end

export function seedRow(a) do
  for each v in nodes() do unmark(v) end     // light only the row we are filling
  mark(a, "info")
  scrollTo(a)
  for each b in nodes() do
    if a = b then
      dist[idx[a]][idx[b]] ← 0              // a vertex reaches itself in 0
    else
      dist[idx[a]][idx[b]] ← weight(a, b)   // a direct edge, or ∞ if there is none
    end
  end
  showMessage("Row " + a + ": direct edges out of " + a + ", ∞ elsewhere", "info")
end

export function relaxThrough(k) do
  for each v in nodes() do unmark(v) end     // highlight only the current intermediate
  mark(k, "info")                            // paths may now hop through k
  scrollTo(k)
  showMessage("Allow paths through " + k, "info")
  for each i in nodes() do
    for each j in nodes() do
      detour ← dist[idx[i]][idx[k]] + dist[idx[k]][idx[j]]
      if detour < dist[idx[i]][idx[j]] then
        dist[idx[i]][idx[j]] ← detour        // i → k → j is shorter — take it
      end
    end
  end
end

export function report() do
  for each v in nodes() do unmark(v) end      // clear the canvas — the table is the result
  unspotlight(dist)
  showMessage("All-pairs shortest distances complete — read dist[i][j]", "success")
end

notes.text: A matrix is indexed by integers, so each vertex gets a number in the **idx** map (`A→0, B→1, …`). It's a `scratch` structure — pure bookkeeping, hidden from the panel — because the next line gives the matrix readable headers instead.

`panel.createMatrix(n, n, …)` makes an `n × n` grid of zeros, off-canvas but watchable in the data panel. We `pin` and `spotlight` it because in Floyd–Warshall the table, not the canvas, is where the work happens.
`setLabels(nodes())` names the rows and columns by vertex, so the panel shows **A, B, C…** along both edges instead of `0, 1, 2…` — the table reads like a distance chart, no separate legend needed.
One step per row keeps the setup visible instead of filling the whole table at once. Vertex `a` is highlighted on the canvas so you can match the row to the vertex it belongs to.
The base case: a vertex reaches **itself** in `0`, a **direct edge** costs its weight, and every other pair is **∞** — `weight` returns ∞ when there is no edge. Each cell blinks as it is written.
The heart of the algorithm. With `k` fixed (highlighted on the canvas), we sweep **every** ordered pair `(i, j)` — that triple loop is why Floyd–Warshall is `O(V³)`.

The relaxation: the best `i → j` path allowed to pass through `k` is either what we already had, or the detour `i → k` then `k → j`. Keep the smaller. ∞ arithmetic does the right thing — an unreachable leg leaves the detour at ∞.
Nothing to highlight on the canvas at the end, so unmark every vertex and drop the spotlight — the completed matrix speaks for itself.
